content: Creativity is fundamentally a recombination process, not an act of conjuring something from nothing. Every novel idea is a collision between existing frames of reference — which means the richness of what you produce is directly constrained by the richness of what you consume. Upgrading your inputs is therefore the most reliable lever for upgrading your outputs.
implication: Before trying to think harder or brainstorm longer, audit your input diet. If your reading, conversations, and experiences are narrow, your ideas will be narrow — no amount of effort compensates for a thin input pool.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)

content: Utley describes what he calls a 'Wonder Wander' — deliberately taking a problem out into the physical world and forcing it to collide with whatever you encounter. A stoplight, a nail salon, a hardware store display all become provocations that the brain would never generate on its own. The brain left alone with itself only recirculates what it already knows.
implication: When stuck, don't push harder at the desk. Take the problem for a walk with the explicit instruction to notice what the environment makes you think of. Scheduled exposure to randomness is a genuine creative technique, not a distraction.
source_ref: The Reason You Don't Have Better Ideas — video

content: Utley argues that the instruction 'think of something new' is operationally useless because it gives the brain no mechanism to work with. The more productive instruction is 'think of connections' — because connections can be engineered by deliberately varying the inputs the brain is exposed to. Reframing creativity as connection-finding transforms it from a mysterious talent into a systematic practice.
implication: Replace vague creative directives in your team with connection prompts: 'What does this problem remind you of in another field?' or 'What would a hotel designer do here?' The mechanism matters more than the aspiration.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)

content: Utley points to the practice of learning from anyone — not just experts in your field — as a core input strategy. The insight is that expertise in an adjacent domain often contains structural lessons that transfer directly to your problem, but only if you are approaching the encounter with a question already in mind. A master potter and a logistics executive may be solving the same underlying problem with different vocabulary.
implication: Before any meeting, conversation, or conference, load a live problem into working memory. Then approach every person you meet as a potential unexpected source of insight on that problem. The question you carry determines what you are able to hear.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Utley documents a recurring pattern: breakthroughs reliably come from outside the domain in which the problem lives. The people solving your industry's hardest problems often come from adjacent fields precisely because they are not constrained by the assumptions your field treats as given. Seeking discomfort through cross-disciplinary exposure is not a soft enrichment activity — it is a structural creativity advantage.
implication: Deliberately schedule exposure to fields that feel irrelevant: attend a talk in a discipline you know nothing about, read a trade publication from an unrelated industry, invite someone with zero context on your problem into the room. Irrelevance is often the signal that maximum transfer value is present.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: Utley describes the 'what does this make me think of?' flip as his signature move for activating associative rather than evaluative thinking. When you ask 'what do I think of this?' you enter a critical posture. When you ask 'what does this make me think of?' you enter a generative posture. The linguistic micro-reframe changes the cognitive mode without any additional effort.
implication: Train your team — and yourself — to default to the associative question first when encountering any new idea, problem, or input. Evaluation has its place, but it should never be the first cognitive move when the goal is creative expansion.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Unconventional Paths to Breakthroughs — video

content: Utley argues that most organizations have a generation problem masquerading as a selection problem. They build sophisticated frameworks for evaluating and filtering ideas, but apply those frameworks to an impoverished input pipeline. A brilliant filter applied to a thin idea pool still produces mediocre results. The highest leverage point is almost always upstream of evaluation — in expanding the volume and variety of raw material entering the funnel.
implication: Before investing in better decision frameworks or innovation scorecards, ask whether the pool of ideas being evaluated is actually large and diverse enough. If not, the selection process is irrelevant — you need a generation intervention first.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)

content: In his essay on surprising patterns, Utley documents that the people who produce the most generative AI outputs are not those with the most technical sophistication but those with the richest and most varied inputs — about themselves, their domain, and adjacent fields. AI amplifies what you bring to it; if what you bring is narrow, the amplification only makes the narrowness louder.
implication: Treat AI as a diagnostic on your own input quality. If the outputs feel generic, the problem is almost never the model — it is the poverty of the context, examples, and perspectives you are feeding it. Richer inputs produce richer amplification.
source_ref: A Surprising Pattern Has Emerged in My Work — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Utley uses the metaphor of Lego bricks to describe creative raw material: the more pieces you have, and the more varied their shapes and colors, the more structures you can build. Someone with a limited set of identical bricks cannot produce novel constructions no matter how creative they are. The constraint is the inventory, not the builder.
implication: Actively expanding your 'Lego inventory' — through books outside your field, conversations with people who challenge your assumptions, and deliberate exposure to unfamiliar contexts — is a form of creative capital investment with compounding returns.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley and Perry Klebahn | Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters | Talks at Google — video

content: Utley identifies 'productive procrastination' as a legitimate and undervalued input strategy. The period of seemingly doing nothing — reading tangentially, wandering, letting the mind drift across unrelated domains — is when the brain is actually performing its most important connection-making work. Scheduling this kind of incubation time is not indulgence; it is an evidence-backed input practice.
implication: Protect white space in your calendar not as recovery time but as input-processing time. The creative breakthroughs that feel like they arrived in the shower were built from inputs that needed time to settle and combine — which means cutting all slack from the schedule cuts the creative pipeline.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on The Power of Productive Procrastination — video

content: Utley points to historical exemplars — Picasso, Edison, Linus Pauling — not primarily as proof that quantity leads to quality, but as proof that all of them were voracious, eclectic consumers of inputs across disciplines. Pauling's Nobel-winning chemistry insights were fed by physics and biology; Picasso absorbed African art and transformed cubism. Volume of output was downstream of volume and diversity of input.
implication: Study the input habits of prolific creators, not just their output habits. The lesson from the masters is less about working harder and more about consuming more widely — which is a more actionable and accessible practice for most people.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, Perry and Utley (2022)

content: Utley describes AI as a new kind of input multiplier: it can expose you to angles, counterarguments, analogies from other fields, and framings you would not have encountered on your own within the time available. Used well, AI dramatically expands the effective input surface of any creative session — not by replacing human judgment but by serving as a rapid cross-domain synthesizer.
implication: Use AI explicitly as an input-expansion tool before creative sessions, not just an output-generation tool after them. Ask it to provide perspectives from unrelated fields, historical analogies, or steelman arguments before you begin generating solutions.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity — video

content: Utley argues that the habit genius researchers most consistently identify is not output behavior — it is a pattern of consuming widely and then giving ideas time to incubate before evaluation. The creative geniuses who appear to do nothing are in fact doing the most important work: loading their associative memory with rich, varied material and allowing unconscious synthesis to operate on it.
implication: Reframe your reading and exploration habits as core professional responsibilities, not discretionary enrichment. The time investment in consuming broadly is the upstream cause of downstream creative output quality.
source_ref: The Habit That Built Every Genius (It Looks Like Doing Nothing) — video

content: Utley's concept of 'carrying a question' into the world transforms ordinary experience into a continuous input stream. Rather than consuming inputs passively, you pre-load a live problem into working memory before any experience — a walk, a dinner conversation, a museum visit — and your brain automatically pattern-matches everything you encounter against that problem. The question you bring determines the creative fuel you harvest.
implication: Before any scheduled experience or interaction, take thirty seconds to load your current most important question. This simple habit converts background noise into active input and dramatically increases the yield of everyday life as a creative resource.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Utley makes the case that exposure to discomfort — deliberately seeking out contexts where you are the least knowledgeable person in the room — is not a soft leadership virtue but a structural input strategy. Discomfort signals that you are encountering genuinely new material, which is the only kind of input capable of producing genuinely new output. Comfortable inputs produce incremental ideas; uncomfortable inputs produce disruptive ones.
implication: Institutionalize discomfort as a design constraint in your learning diet. If every source you consume confirms what you already believe, you are refining existing ideas rather than generating new ones. Seek out the room, the book, or the conversation that makes you feel least qualified.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley — podcast

content: Utley describes design thinking's core diagnostic insight: most innovation failures are not execution failures but input failures — teams that did not talk to enough diverse people, observe enough edge cases, or immerse themselves deeply enough in the actual lived experience of the problem. Better fieldwork upstream prevents the expensive failure of beautifully executing the wrong solution.
implication: Before any ideation session, ask: how many different perspectives have we actually absorbed? If the team has only talked to five customers who look like each other, the input pool is too narrow to produce non-obvious solutions, regardless of the quality of the ideation process.
source_ref: Stanford Webinar - Design Thinking: What is it and why should I care? — video

content: Utley notes a paradox in how people approach AI: they want it to produce novel ideas but give it only the most familiar, generic context. The model can only recombine what it is given in the conversation window. Feeding it richer, stranger, more specific inputs — a transcript from an unrelated field, a provocative constraint, an analogous problem from history — produces proportionally richer outputs.
implication: Treat the AI conversation window as a creative staging area, not a search bar. The quality of what you put in — the specificity, the range of reference material, the provocative framing — is the primary determinant of the quality of what comes out.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas — interview

content: Utley observes that the people who learn fastest are those who approach every interaction — not just formal learning contexts — as a potential input source. The pattern he has documented is that breakthrough innovators treat taxi drivers, strangers at conferences, and overheard conversations as raw material, not noise. The input diet never goes off duty for people who have made curiosity a structural habit rather than an occasional mood.
implication: Curiosity is not a personality trait but a practice. Formalizing it — through journaling, question-carrying, or end-of-day reflection on what surprised you — converts an intermittent mood into a reliable input engine.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Utley consistently frames the Stanford d.school's emphasis on observational fieldwork — watching real people struggle with real problems in real contexts — as the most underrated input practice in innovation. Observation done well is not just data collection; it is a form of perspective transplant that restructures the observer's assumptions from the inside, making certain solution directions feel obviously right that would never have emerged from desk research.
implication: Replace some of your secondary research time with direct observation. One hour watching someone actually use your product, navigate your service, or work around the problem you are trying to solve generates more generative input than ten hours of survey analysis.
source_ref: Stanford Webinar - Design Thinking: What is it and why should I care? — video

content: Utley argues that the scarcest input in most organizations is genuine dissent and honest challenge. Leaders who surround themselves with confirming voices are limiting their input diet to a single flavor — which produces ideas that are safe, familiar, and incremental by structural necessity. Actively engineering disagreement into your input stream is as important as diversifying industries or disciplines.
implication: Audit not just what you read and who you follow, but whether anyone in your environment is consistently challenging your assumptions. If not, the input monoculture is a creativity liability, regardless of how much content you are consuming.
source_ref: Episode 259 – Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley — podcast

content: Utley draws a direct connection between input diversity and the phenomenon he calls 'exponential ideaflow' in the AI era. When AI can rapidly synthesize inputs from across disciplines, the creative ceiling is no longer set by how broadly a single human has read — it is set by how broadly and specifically you can feed the AI. This means the human's job shifts from being a walking encyclopedia to being a skilled input curator and director.
implication: In an AI-augmented creative workflow, curation skill becomes more valuable than encyclopedic knowledge. Learning to identify, select, and feed high-quality, diverse inputs to an AI partner is the new creative leverage — and it is a learnable skill, not a fixed trait.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity — video

content: Utley connects the concept of input quality directly to ideaflow measurement: teams with high ideaflow almost always have deliberately varied input practices — they read widely, interview edge-case users, and seek out perspectives that challenge their assumptions. Teams with low ideaflow tend to have homogeneous input habits regardless of how hard they work during ideation sessions. The session itself is too late in the process to compensate for input poverty.
implication: Measure and manage input diversity as a leading indicator of ideaflow, just as ideaflow is a leading indicator of innovation output. Ask: over the past month, what genuinely new perspectives has this team been exposed to? The answer predicts next quarter's idea quality.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley — podcast

content: Utley identifies a specific failure mode he sees repeatedly in corporate innovation efforts: teams that restrict their reference points to their own industry. They study only competitors, benchmark only against peers, and hire only people with directly relevant experience. The result is the creative equivalent of inbreeding — small, incremental variations on existing forms because the entire input gene pool is too narrow.
implication: Actively seek benchmark comparisons and input sources from industries structurally analogous to yours but superficially unrelated. Fast food logistics, military decision-making, film production, emergency medicine — all contain operational wisdom that transfers with high creative yield across sector boundaries.
source_ref: Maximizing the Flow of Ideas for Your Organization — interview
